name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا نَزَلَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلُ فَطَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for Allah’s remembrance and toward the truth which has come down [to them], and to be not like those who were given the Book before? Time took its toll on them and so their hearts were hardened, and many of them are transgressors. (16)
```

```arabic
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
Know that Allah revives the earth after its death. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, so that you may exercise your reason. (17)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُصَّدِّقَـٰتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١٨)
```

```english
Indeed the charitable men and women and those who lend Allah a good loan—it shall be multiplied for them, and there will be a noble reward for them. (18)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصِّدِّيقُونَ ۖ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٩)
```

```english
Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and witnesses with their Lord; they shall have their reward and their light. But as for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (19)
```

```arabic
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفَاخُرٌۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكَاثُرٌ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ ۖ كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ ٱلْكُفَّارَ نَبَاتُهُۥ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطَـٰمًا ۖ وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ ۚ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ (٢٠)
```

```english
Know that the life of this world is mere diversion and play, glamour and mutual vainglory among you and rivalry for wealth and children—like rain, whose growth impresses the farmer. Then it withers and you see it turn yellow, then it becomes chaff. Whereas in the Hereafter there is forgiveness from Allah and His approval and a severe punishment. The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion. (20)
```

```arabic
سَابِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢١)
```

```english
Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (21)
```

```arabic
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٢٢)
```

```english
No affliction visits the land or yourselves but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah— (22)
```

```arabic
لِّكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (٢٣)
```

```english
so that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor boast for what comes your way, and Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (23)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٤)
```

```english
Such as are [themselves] stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy. And whoever refuses to comply [should know that] indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (24)
```

 thoughtful
## (Commentary)

The discourse proceeds in accordance with the objective of the preceding speech, which is to urge and encourage faith in Allah and His Messenger, and spending in the way of Allah. It includes a reproach of the believers for the signs of hard-heartedness appearing among them, and reinforces the encouragement to spend by clarifying the rank of the spenders with Allah, commanding them to race toward forgiveness and Paradise, and censuring the world and its people who are parsimonious and command others to be parsimonious.

The context shifts during these verses to a general address encompassing both the Muslims and the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), after the previous context was exclusive to the Muslims; the clarification of this will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللهِ وَما نَزَلَ مِنَ الْحَقِ **"Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for the remembrance of Allah and the truth that has come down [to them]..."** (57:16) until the end of the verse. It is said: *ana ya’ni inan wa ina’an* (أنى يأني إنى وإناء), meaning its time has arrived. The humbling (خشوع) of the heart is its being affected in the presence of grandeur and majesty. What is meant by "the remembrance of Allah" (ذكر الله) is that by which Allah is remembered, and "the truth that has come down" (ما نزل من الحق) is the Quran revealed from Him, the Exalted; "of the truth" (مِنَ الْحَقِ) is an explanation of what was revealed. It is the nature of the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, for a believer that it should be followed by humility, just as it is the nature of the truth revealed from Him, the Exalted, that it should be followed by humility from one who believes in Allah and His messengers.

It has been said that "the remembrance of Allah" and "the truth that has come down" both refer to the Quran. According to this view, the Quran is mentioned by two of its attributes because each attribute necessitates the humility of the believer; the Quran, by virtue of being the remembrance of Allah, demands humility, just as it demands humility by virtue of being the truth revealed from Him, the Exalted.

The verse contains a reproach for the believers regarding the hardness (القسوة) that had overcome their hearts and their lack of humility toward the remembrance of Allah and the truth revealed from Him. It likens their state to that of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) — *those to whom a scripture was previously revealed* — upon whom time was prolonged and thus their hearts became hardened.

His saying: وَلا يَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَطالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ **"...and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before: time was prolonged for them and so their hearts hardened..."** (57:16) is conjoined to His saying "should be humbled" (تَخْشَعَ). The meaning is: Is it not time for them that their hearts should be humbled and that they should not be, etc. The term *al-amad* (الأمد) means time (الزمان). Al-Raghib said: "The difference between *al-zaman* (الزمان) and *al-amad* (الأمد) is that *al-amad* is used in consideration of the end-point (الغاية), while *al-zaman* is general regarding the beginning and the end. For this reason, some have said that *al-mada* (المدى) and *al-amad* are close in meaning." End quote.

He, the Glorified, has pointed out with this speech that their hearts were becoming like the hardened hearts of the People of the Book. When the hard heart (القلب القاسي) loses humility and the capacity to be affected by the truth, it may depart from the attire of servitude (زي العبودية), failing to be moved by prohibitions and committing sin and transgression (الفسوق). Therefore, He followed His saying "and so their hearts hardened" with His saying: وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ **"...and many of them are transgressors."** (57:16).

**His saying, the Exalted:** اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها **"Know that Allah revives the earth after its death..."** (57:17) until the end of the verse. Following the reproach of the believers for the hardness of their hearts with this parable serves to strengthen their hope and encourage them toward humility.

It is possible that this is a completion of the previous reproach, serving as a reminder that Allah does not leave this religion in such a state; rather, whenever hearts harden and are deprived of humility toward Allah's command, He brings forth hearts that are alive and humble before Him, through which He is worshipped as He desires.

Thus, the verse would be similar in meaning to His saying: ها أَنْتُمْ هؤُلاءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يَبْخَلُ وَمَنْ يَبْخَلْ فَإِنَّما يَبْخَلُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَاللهُ الْغَنِيُّ وَأَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ وَإِنْ تَتَوَلَّوْا يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْماً غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَكُونُوا أَمْثالَكُمْ **"Ah! You are the ones who are invited to spend in the way of Allah; yet among you are those who are stingy; and whoever is stingy is stingy only to himself. Allah is the All-sufficient, and you are the needy. And if you turn away, He will replace you with another people, and they will not be like you."** (Surah Muhammad: 38).

For this reason, He concluded the verse by saying: قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ **"We have certainly made the signs clear for you, so that you may exercise your reason."** (57:17).

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنَّ الْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَالْمُصَّدِّقاتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً يُضاعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ **"Indeed the charitable men and women and those who lend Allah a good loan—it shall be multiplied for them, and there is a noble reward for them."** (57:18). This is a repetition of the discussion regarding multiplication and the noble reward (الأجر الكريم) to encourage spending in the way of Allah. To those who lend Allah a good loan, He added "the charitable men and women" (المصدقون والمصدقات).

The terms *al-mussaddiqun* and *al-mussaddiqat* (المصدقون والمصدقات) — with a shadda on the *sad* and *dal* — mean those who give charity (المتصدقون والمتصدقات). His saying "and those who lend Allah" (وَأَقْرَضُوا اللهَ) is conjoined to the noun governed by the definite article in "the charitable" (الْمُصَّدِّقِينَ). The meaning is: Indeed, those who give charity and those who lend Allah a good loan shall have what they gave multiplied for them, and they shall have a noble reward.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ **"Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord..."** (57:19) etc. He did not say "faith in Allah and His Messenger" as He said at the beginning of the Surah: "Believe in Allah and His Messenger and spend," and as He said at its end: "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Messenger." This is because when He mentioned the People of the Book in the previous verse by saying "and that they should not be like those who were given the Book before," He shifted from the previous context to a general context that includes both the Muslims and the People of the Book together, as He says later: "Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs." As for the two verses mentioned at the beginning and end of the Surah, the address in them is specifically for the believers of this Ummah; hence, the Messenger is mentioned in the singular.

What is meant by "faith in Allah and His messengers" is pure faith (الإيمان) — *the firm conviction that by its nature does not depart from obedience and following* — as previously indicated in His saying: "Believe in Allah and His Messenger." What is meant by His saying "it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses" is their being joined to the ranks of the truthful (الصديقون) and the witnesses (الشهداء), as evidenced by His saying "with their Lord" and His saying "they shall have their reward and their light." Thus, they are joined to these two groups and treated as the truthful and the witnesses, being given the like of their reward and their light.

It appears that what is meant by "the truthful" (الصديقون) and "the witnesses" (الشهداء) are those mentioned in His saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً **"Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the witnesses and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!"** (4:69). It has already been mentioned in the commentary of that verse that "the truthful" (الصديقون) are those whose truthfulness permeates their speech and action, so they do what they say and say what they do. "The witnesses" (الشهداء) are the witnesses over deeds on the Day of Resurrection, not "martyrs" in the sense of those killed in the way of Allah.

So, these who believe in Allah and His messengers are joined to the truthful and the witnesses, placed in their station with Allah — that is, by His decree — they shall have their reward and their light.

In His saying: لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ **"...they shall have their reward and their light"** (57:19), the pronoun "they" (لَهُمْ) refers to those who believe, while the pronoun in "their reward and their light" (أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ) refers to the truthful and the witnesses. That is, those who believe shall have a reward of the type of reward given to the truthful and the witnesses, and a light of the type of their light. This is the meaning intended by those who said the meaning is: they have a reward like their reward and a light like their light.

It has been suggested that the verse is intended to state that they are truthful and witnesses in reality, without any sense of joining or being placed in a station; they are indeed them, and thus have their reward and light. However, the context perhaps does not support this.

It has also been suggested that His saying "and the witnesses" (وَالشُّهَداءُ) is not conjoined to His saying "the truthful" (الصِّدِّيقُونَ), but is rather a new sentence (استئناف). Thus, "the witnesses" is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is "with their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ), and its other predicate is "they shall have their reward" (لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ). It is said: "Those who have faith in Allah and His messengers—it is they who are the truthful," and the speech is complete. Then it begins anew: "And the witnesses are with their Lord," just as it is said: "rather they are alive with their Lord" (3:169). In this view, "the witnesses" refers to those killed in the way of Allah. Then the speech is completed with His saying: "they shall have their reward and their light."

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ **"As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell."** (57:19). That is, they shall not depart from it, remaining therein forever.

In this verse, He, the Glorified, has addressed the state of those joined to the truthful and the witnesses — who are the best of people and certainly saved — and the disbelievers who deny His signs — who are the worst of people and certainly perished. There remains a group between the two: the believers who commit sins and offenses, according to their various levels of rebellion against Allah and His Messenger. This is the custom of the Quran in many instances when addressing the state of people on the Day of Resurrection.

This is done to stimulate the faculties of fear (الخوف) and hope (الرجاء) in that intermediate group between the best and the worst, so they may incline toward felicity and choose salvation over destruction.

For this reason, He followed the verse with a censure of the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا), to which those who refuse to spend in the way of Allah are attached. Then He followed it with an invitation to race toward forgiveness and Paradise, and then by pointing out that whatever affliction (مصيبة) befalls them in their wealth or themselves is written in a prior Book and a preceding decree (قضاء). Therefore, they should not fear poverty when spending in the way of Allah, leading them to be stingy and withhold, nor should they fear death in Jihad in the way of Allah, leading them to stay behind and remain idle.

**His saying, the Exalted:** اعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفاخُرٌ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكاثُرٌ فِي الْأَمْوالِ وَالْأَوْلادِ **"Know that the life of this world is but play and diversion, and adornment and boasting among you, and vying in wealth and children..."** (57:20) etc. **Play** (اللعب) is an organized action for an imaginary purpose, like the play of children. **Diversion** (اللهو) is that which occupies a person away from what is important to him. **Adornment** (الزينة) is a type of construction, and it may refer to that which one uses to beautify oneself; it is the joining of something desirable to another thing so that the latter becomes desirable through the beauty it has acquired. **Boasting** (التفاخر) is prideful display of lineage and status. **Vying** (التكاثر) is the competition in wealth and children.

The life of this world is a fleeting commodity (عرض زائل) and a false mirage (سراب باطل), never free from these five mentioned characteristics: play, diversion, adornment, boasting, and vying. These are the things to which the desire of the human soul attaches, either to some or all of them. They are illusory matters and fleeting accidents that do not remain with the human being; not one of them brings the human being any spiritual perfection (كمال نفسي) or true good (خير حقيقي).

It is narrated from our teacher al-Baha’i (may Allah have mercy on him) that these five characteristics mentioned in the verse are ordered according to the years of a person's life and the stages of his existence. First, he is obsessed with play while a child or adolescent. Then, when he reaches maturity and his frame strengthens, he becomes attached to diversion and amusements. Then, when he reaches full strength, he occupies himself with adornment in the form of fine clothing, elegant mounts, and lofty dwellings, becoming infatuated with grace and beauty. Then, when he reaches middle age, he takes to boasting of status and lineage. Finally, when he becomes old, he strives in vying for wealth and children.

His saying: كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ الْكُفَّارَ نَباتُهُ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَراهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطاماً **"...like the example of a rain whose growth impresses the tillers; then it withers and you see it turn yellow, then it becomes chaff."** (57:20). This is a parable for the adornment of the life of this world, to which a person attaches himself out of delusion, only for it to be stripped away shortly thereafter.

*Al-ghayth* (الغيث) is rain. *Al-kuffar* (الكفار) is the plural of *kafir* in the sense of a tiller (الحارث). *Yahiju* (يهيج) is from *al-hayajan*, which is movement. *Al-hutam* (الحطام) is dry, broken-up vegetation.

The meaning is: The likeness of the life of this world in its impressive splendor and then its disappearance is like rain whose resulting vegetation impresses the tillers; then it moves toward the limit of its growth, then you see it turned yellow in color, then it becomes broken-up chaff — disintegrated and scattered by the winds.

His saying: وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللهِ وَرِضْوانٌ **"...while in the Hereafter there is a severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and [His] pleasure..."** (57:20). Forgiveness (المغفرة) precedes pleasure (الرضوان) to purify the locus so that pleasure may reside therein. Describing forgiveness as being "from Allah" while not doing so for the punishment is not without an intimation that what is intended by primary purpose is forgiveness. As for punishment, it is not intended for its own sake; rather, the human being causes it for himself by departing from the attire of servitude, as has been said.

His saying: وَمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا مَتاعُ الْغُرُورِ **"...and the life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion."** (57:20). That is, it is a commodity whose enjoyment is itself the delusion by it. This applies to the one who is attached and deluded by it.

The speech — namely His saying: "while in the Hereafter there is a severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and pleasure" — is an indication of the two aspects of the Hereafter so that the listener may take precaution and choose forgiveness and pleasure over punishment. Then, in His saying: "and the life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion," there is a warning and an awakening so that the life of this world does not delude him with its specific deception.

**His saying, the Exalted:** سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُها كَعَرْضِ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ **"Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise whose width is like the width of the sky and the earth..."** (57:21) etc. **Racing** (المسابقة) is the striving to outdo another in reaching a goal, such that each racer intends to make his movement faster than that of his companion. Thus, the meaning of racing (المسابقة) contains more than the meaning of hastening (المسارعة); for hastening is the effort to speed up one's movement, while racing is the effort to speed it up such that it exceeds the speed of one's companion.

Accordingly, His saying "Take the lead towards forgiveness" (سابِقُوا إِلى مَغْفِرَةٍ) etc., involves a duty that is greater than what is involved in His saying: "Hasten towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise whose width is [as] the heavens and the earth, prepared for the God-conscious" (3:133).

From this, the incorrectness of what has been said becomes apparent: that the verse in Al-Imran is regarding the foremost ones (السابقون) — *those brought near to Allah* — while the verse we are currently discussing is regarding the general believers, since it mentions nothing but faith in Allah and His messengers, unlike the verse in Al-Imran which is followed by a mention of righteous deeds. For this reason also, the Paradise promised there is described by His saying "whose width is [as] the heavens and the earth," unlike here where it is said "whose width is like the width of the sky and the earth," suggesting that the Paradise of those is wider than the Paradise of these.

The reason for this incorrectness is what you have known: that the duty in the verse under discussion is a meaning beyond what was commanded in the verse of Al-Imran. Furthermore, the definite article in "the sky" (السَّماءِ) is for the genus, so it corresponds to "the heavens" (السَّماواتُ) in that verse.

The precedence of forgiveness over Paradise in the verse is because life in Paradise is a pure life in the realm of purity (عالم الطهارة), so being clothed in it depends on the removal of the impurities and filth of sins.

What is meant by "width" (العرض) is vastness (السعة), not the width that is the opposite of length, which is a common usage. The speech is as if intended to indicate its ultimate vastness.

It has been said that "width" refers to that which is opposite to length, and mentioning only the width is more eloquent than mentioning length with it; for width is the shorter of the two dimensions, and if it is like the width of the sky and the earth, then its length must be greater than their length.

This view is not free from arbitrariness, as there is no evidence for the equality of the length of the sky and the earth to their width, nor for the increase of the length of Paradise over its width such that it would necessitate the length of Paradise exceeding their length. Length may equal width, as in a square, a circle, the surface of a sphere, and others, or it may exceed it.

His saying: أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ **"...prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles."** (57:21). You have already known in the discussion of "faith in Allah and His messengers" and "for those who have faith in Allah and His messengers" that what is meant by faith in Allah and His messengers is a high degree of faith that necessitates the occurrence of its effects, such as righteous deeds and the avoidance of transgression and sin.

By this, it becomes clear that the statement of some — that in the verse is a glad tiding for the general believers since He said "prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles" without conditioning faith with any righteous deed or the like — is not sound. For although the address of the verse in its literal wording encompasses the disbeliever and the believer, the righteous and the wicked, the direction of the speech is toward the believers, calling them to the faith that is accompanied by righteous deeds. If what was meant by faith in Allah and His messengers was mere faith even if not accompanied by righteous deeds, and Paradise were prepared for them and the verse were calling to race toward forgiveness and Paradise, then the address "Take the lead" (سابِقُوا) would be directed toward the disbelievers, for the believers would have already taken the lead; the context of the verses rejects this.

His saying: ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ **"That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes..."** (57:21). And He has wished to grant it to those who believe in Allah and His messengers. It has already been explained that what Allah grants of reward to His believing servants is a grace (فضل) from Him, the Exalted, without them having a right to it over Him.

His saying: وَاللهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ **"...and Allah is the dispenser of a great grace."** (57:21) is an indication of the greatness of His grace, and that what He rewards them with of forgiveness and Paradise is from His great grace.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَمْوالِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها **"No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about..."** (57:22) etc. An **affliction** (المصيبة) is an occurrence that hits a thing, derived from an arrow hitting its target. Conceptually, it is more general than good or evil, but its usage has become predominant in evil; thus, an affliction is a calamity (النائبة).

The affliction that visits the earth is like drought, blight of fruits, destructive earthquakes, and the like. That which visits the souls is like illness, wounding, fracture, killing, and death. **Bringing about** (البرء والبروء) is creation from non-existence. The pronoun in "We bring it about" (نَبْرَأَها) refers to the affliction. It has been said it refers to the souls, or the earth, or all of them — the earth, the souls, and the affliction. The first view is supported by the fact that the context is one of explaining the afflictions in the world that cause a decrease in wealth and souls, which invite them to withhold from spending and stay behind from Jihad.

What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب) is the Tablet (اللوح) in which is written what was, what will be, and what is occurring until the Day of Resurrection, as indicated by verses and narrations. He restricted the mention to what visits the earth and their souls of afflictions because the discourse is centered on them.

It has been said: He only restricted the affliction to what is in the earth and in the souls because absolute afflictions are not written in the Tablet, for the Tablet is finite while events are infinite, and the finite cannot be a vessel for the infinite.

This speech is based on the idea that what is meant by the Tablet is a metallic or similar plate stationed in some region of the atmosphere, in which events are written in one of our languages with a script resembling our scripts. Discussion regarding the meaning of the Tablet (اللوح) and the Pen (القلم) has already passed, and its completion will follow.

It has also been said: What is meant by the Book is His knowledge, the Exalted. This is contrary to the literal meaning, unless it is meant that the Book in which events are written is one of the levels of His active knowledge (علمه الفعلي).

He concluded the verse with His saying: إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ **"...that is indeed easy for Allah"** (57:22) to indicate that the predetermination of events before their occurrence and decreeing them with an unchangeable decree involves no difficulty for Him, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ **"So that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor exult for what He has granted you..."** (57:23) etc. This is a rationale (تعليل) referring back to the previous verse; it is a rationale for the *informing* about the writing of events before their occurrence, not for the writing itself. **Grief** (الأسى) is sadness. What is meant by "what escapes" and "what He has granted" is the escaped blessing and the granted blessing.

The meaning is: We have informed you of the writing of events before their happening and realization so that you may not be saddened by the blessings that escape you, nor exult by those He has given you. For when a person is certain that what has befallen him was predetermined and bound to happen, and could not have missed him, and that the blessings he was given are a trust with him until a specified term, his sadness when they escape him will not be great, nor will his joy when they are given to him.

It has been said: The difference in attribution in His sayings "what escapes you" (ما فاتَكُمْ) and "what He has granted you" (بِما آتاكُمْ) — where He attributed the escaping to the things themselves and the granting to Allah, the Glorified — is because escaping and non-existence are intrinsic to things; if left to themselves, they would not remain. This is in contrast to their acquisition and survival, which must be attributed to Allah, the Exalted.

His saying: وَاللهُ لا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتالٍ فَخُورٍ **"...and Allah does not love any swaggering braggart"** (57:23). The **swaggerer** (المختال) is one seized by *al-khuyala’* (الخيلاء), which is arrogance arising from the imagination of a virtue that appears to him from himself — as mentioned by al-Raghib. The **braggart** (الفخور) is one who frequently boasts and displays pride. Swaggering and boasting arise from a person's delusion that he owns the blessings he was given by his own merit. This is contrary to the truth, which is the attribution of that to a predetermination from Allah, not to an independence of the human soul; thus, they are among the vices, and Allah does not love them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبُخْلِ **"such as are stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy."** (57:24). This is a description of every swaggering braggart, providing the rationale for His not loving them. The reason for their stinginess is the preservation of the wealth upon which their swaggering and boasting depend. The reason for their bidding people to be stingy is that they love it for themselves and thus love it for others, and because the prevalence of generosity and munificence among people and their inclination toward spending in the way of Allah would cause them to be recognized for their blameworthy stinginess.

His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ **"And whoever turns away, then Allah is indeed the All-sufficient, the All-laudable."** (57:24). That is, whoever turns away from spending and is not admonished by Allah's admonition, nor is his heart reassured by what He has explained of the attributes of the world, the description of Paradise, and the predetermination of affairs, then Allah is the All-sufficient (الغني) — He has no need for their spending — and He is the All-laudable (الحميد) in His acts.

The three verses — namely from His saying "No affliction visits" until His saying "the All-sufficient, the All-laudable" — are, as you see, an encouragement to spend and a deterrent against stinginess and withholding, by making them indifferent to grieving over what escapes them and exulting over what He grants them, because affairs are predetermined, decreed, and written in a specific Book before Allah, the Glorified, brings them about.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ **"Is it not time..."** (57:16) the verse: Ibn al-Mubarak, Abd al-Razzaq, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from al-A’mash who said: "When the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله arrived in Medina — and they attained the ease of life they attained, after the hardship they had been in — they seemed to slacken in some of what they were upon. So they were reproached, and there was revealed: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا **'Is it not time yet for those who have faith...'**"

**I say:** This is the most balanced of the narrations regarding the revelation of the Surah. There is a narration from Ibn Mas’ud who said: "There was nothing between our Islam and Allah reproaching us with this verse: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللهِ **'Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for the remembrance of Allah...'** except four years." Its literal sense is that the Surah is Meccan. Similar to this is what has been reported that Umar believed after the revelation of this Surah. You have already known that the context of the Surah's verses insists that it must be Medinan. It is possible to interpret the narration of Ibn Mas’ud as referring to the verse "Is it not time," etc., or it and the one following it, being among what was revealed in Mecca, unlike the rest of the Surah's verses.

In a narration from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Allah found the hearts of the Emigrants (المهاجرين) to be slow — after seventeen [years] of the Quran's revelation — so Allah revealed: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ **'Is it not time...'** the verse." This necessitates the revelation of the Surah in the fourth or fifth year of the Hijrah. In another narration from Ibn Abbas, he said: "Indeed, Allah found the hearts of the Emigrants to be slow — so He reproached them at the head of thirteen years of the Quran's revelation — and said: أَلَمْ يَأْنِ **'Is it not time...'**" etc. This necessitates the revelation of the Surah during the days of the Hijrah. These two narrations also do not harmonize with the context of its verses.

In it, Ibn Jarir narrated from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: 'The believers of my Ummah are witnesses (شهداء).' Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ ـ وَالشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ **'Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord.'**"

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, with its chain of transmission from Minhal al-Qassab, who said to Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Pray to Allah to grant me martyrdom (الشهادة)." He replied: "Indeed, the believer is a witness (شهيد)," and he recited this verse.

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning, and the literal sense of some of them, like this narration, is the interpretation of "witness" (الشهادة) as being killed in the way of Allah.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of transmission from Hafs ibn Ghiyath, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: 'May I be your ransom — what is the limit of asceticism (الزهد) in the world?' He replied: 'Allah has limited it in His Book, for He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ **"So that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor exult for what He has granted you."**'"

In *Nahj al-Balagha*, he عليه‌السلام said: "Asceticism (الزهد) in its entirety is between two words of the Quran. Allah, the Exalted, said: لِكَيْلا تَأْسَوْا عَلى ما فاتَكُمْ وَلا تَفْرَحُوا بِما آتاكُمْ **'So that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor exult for what He has granted you.'** Whoever does not grieve over the past and does not exult over the coming has taken asceticism by both its ends."

**I say:** The foundation upon which these two rest is the lack of the heart's attachment to the world. In the well-known hadith: "Love of the world is the head of every sin."
